tasks.title: Warm-up: Verify the CNI Environment and Experience the Default-Allow State
tasks.goal: Prepare a cluster with Calico CNI enabled and confirm with curl that Pod-to-Pod communication is allowed by default when no NetworkPolicy exists.
tasks.prompt:
1. Start minikube with the Calico CNI and verify that the Calico Pods in the kube-system namespace are in the Running state.
2. Create the demo namespace.
3. Inside the demo namespace, create a backend Pod labeled app=nginx along with a Service on port 80.
4. Launch a temporary client Pod using the curlimages/curl image, send a curl request to the backend Service, and confirm that the nginx response HTML is returned.

Note: NetworkPolicy enforcement requires a CNI plugin that supports it. This entire lab assumes Calico CNI. If minikube is started without the --cni=calico option (default kindnet), policies are silently ignored — make sure to start with --cni=calico.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Start minikube with Calico CNI
minikube start --cni=calico

# Verify Calico Pods are Running
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep calico

# 2. Create the namespace
kubectl create namespace ____

# 3. Create the backend Pod + Service
kubectl run backend --image=____ --labels="____=____" \
  -n demo --expose --port=____

# 4. Launch a temporary client Pod and curl the backend (a response means default-allow is confirmed)
kubectl run client --image=curlimages/curl -n demo \
  --rm -it -- curl --max-time 5 ____
tasks.solution:
# 1. Start minikube with Calico CNI
minikube start --cni=calico

# Verify Calico Pods — calico-node, calico-kube-controllers, etc. must be Running
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep calico

# 2. Create the namespace
kubectl create namespace demo

# 3. Create the backend Pod + Service (--expose also creates a ClusterIP Service)
kubectl run backend --image=nginx --labels="app=nginx" \
  -n demo --expose --port=80

# Verify creation
kubectl get pod,svc -n demo

# 4. Launch a temporary client Pod and curl the backend
# The Service name 'backend' is resolvable via cluster DNS
kubectl run client --image=curlimages/curl -n demo \
  --rm -it -- curl --max-time 5 backend
# Expected result: <!DOCTYPE html> ... nginx default HTML is printed
# → Confirms that all Pod-to-Pod communication is allowed by default in the flat network
tasks.explanation: Kubernetes adopts a 'flat network model' by default. All Pods can communicate freely with each other regardless of namespace or node. In this state, with no NetworkPolicy objects present, there are no blocking rules either. The nginx response returned by curl is proof of the 'default-allow' state. NetworkPolicy enforcement is performed not by the Kubernetes API server but by the CNI plugin. If an unsupported CNI is used (e.g., minikube's default kindnet), even correctly written NetworkPolicy objects are silently ignored and all traffic continues to flow without any error. Calico, Cilium, and Weave Net are among the CNIs that do enforce NetworkPolicy.
hints: If minikube is already running, delete it with `minikube delete` and start fresh with `minikube start --cni=calico`. Replacing the CNI on an existing cluster is complex, so a clean restart is the safest approach.

In the output of `kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep calico`, the calico-node Pod must appear as Running for each node. If it is in Pending or CrashLoopBackOff, Calico is still initializing — wait 1–2 more minutes.

The `kubectl run backend --expose` command creates both a Pod and a ClusterIP Service with the same name. You can use the Service name `backend` as the curl target because cluster DNS (CoreDNS) automatically resolves `<service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local`.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 1: Apply a Default Deny Ingress Policy and Confirm Traffic Is Blocked
tasks.goal: Apply an ingress default deny policy to all Pods in the namespace using podSelector: {} and directly confirm that the curl response is blocked (times out).
tasks.prompt:
Write a default deny NetworkPolicy (YAML filename: default-deny.yaml) that blocks all inbound traffic for every Pod in the demo namespace, then apply it.

Requirements:
- kind: NetworkPolicy, namespace: demo
- Apply to all Pods: set podSelector to an empty value ({})
- List only Ingress in policyTypes
- Writing no ingress rules at all means all incoming traffic is blocked

After applying, launch the curl client Pod again, attempt to connect to backend, and confirm the request times out with no response.
Also verify the policy was created with kubectl get networkpolicy -n demo.
tasks.starter:
# default-deny.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: ____
metadata:
  name: default-deny-ingress
  namespace: ____
spec:
  podSelector: ____    # empty object: all Pods in the namespace
  policyTypes:
    - ____             # inbound direction only
  # No ingress rules → all incoming traffic is blocked

# Apply
kubectl apply -f default-deny.yaml

# Verify the policy was created
kubectl get networkpolicy -n demo

# Confirm blocking: --max-time 5 sets a 5-second timeout
kubectl run client --image=curlimages/curl -n demo \
  --rm -it -- curl --max-time 5 ____
tasks.solution:
# default-deny.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-ingress
  namespace: demo
spec:
  podSelector: {}       # {} = no label condition = applies to all Pods in the namespace
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  # The ingress key is omitted entirely → 'no allowed inbound' = block all

# Apply
kubectl apply -f default-deny.yaml

# Verify the policy
kubectl get networkpolicy -n demo
# Sample output:
# NAME                   POD-SELECTOR   AGE
# default-deny-ingress   <none>         5s

# Confirm blocking (times out after 5 seconds)
kubectl run client --image=curlimages/curl -n demo \
  --rm -it -- curl --max-time 5 backend
# Expected result: curl: (28) Connection timed out after 5000 milliseconds
# → NetworkPolicy is active and inbound traffic is being blocked
tasks.explanation: NetworkPolicy uses a whitelist (allow-list) model. The moment a policy is applied to a Pod, the rule becomes 'only explicitly allowed traffic passes; everything else is blocked.' podSelector: {} means 'no label condition = select all Pods,' and listing Ingress in policyTypes without writing any ingress rules means 'zero allow rules = block everything.' The timeout on curl is evidence that the policy is actively working. Note the distinction: an immediate 'Connection refused' means the service itself is unavailable, whereas a timeout means the packet reached the network layer but was dropped by the CNI.
hints: In podSelector: {}, the braces must contain nothing. Using podSelector: null or omitting the podSelector key entirely behaves differently — always write the explicit empty object {}.
Specifying only policyTypes: [Ingress] leaves egress unrestricted. Blocking egress at this stage would cut DNS and break name resolution in later steps. Only block Ingress here.
In the output of kubectl get networkpolicy -n demo, the POD-SELECTOR column showing <none> is correct — it represents the empty selector (all Pods) and is expected behavior.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 2: Allow Ingress Only from Pods with a Specific Label
tasks.goal: Write a NetworkPolicy that allows only Pods labeled access=granted to reach port 80 of the app=nginx Pod. Run curl from both a labeled and an unlabeled Pod to compare the allow and block outcomes.
tasks.prompt:
Write and apply a NetworkPolicy (allow-frontend.yaml) satisfying the following conditions.

Requirements:
- Protected target: Pods labeled app=nginx (podSelector)
- Allowed source: Pods labeled access=granted (from.podSelector)
- Allowed port: TCP 80
- policyTypes: Ingress

After applying, test with two different clients and compare results.
(a) A Pod with the access=granted label → curl to backend, confirm the nginx HTML response
(b) A Pod without the label → curl to backend, confirm the request is blocked (timeout)
tasks.starter:
# allow-frontend.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-to-nginx
  namespace: demo
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      ____: ____        # label of the protected target Pod
  policyTypes:
    - ____
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              ____: ____    # label of the allowed source Pod
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: ____

# Apply
kubectl apply -f allow-frontend.yaml

# (a) Client with label → expect connection success
kubectl run good --image=curlimages/curl --labels="____=____" \
  -n demo --rm -it -- curl --max-time 5 backend

# (b) Client without label → expect block (timeout)
kubectl run bad --image=curlimages/curl \
  -n demo --rm -it -- curl --max-time 5 backend
tasks.solution:
# allow-frontend.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-to-nginx
  namespace: demo
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx          # this policy applies to: the backend Pod
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              access: granted   # only allow traffic from Pods with this label
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 80              # only on port 80

# Apply (added on top of the existing default-deny)
kubectl apply -f allow-frontend.yaml

# Verify the current policy list (should show 2 policies)
kubectl get networkpolicy -n demo
# NAME                   POD-SELECTOR   AGE
# allow-to-nginx         app=nginx      5s
# default-deny-ingress   <none>         2m

# (a) Pod with access=granted label → connection succeeds
kubectl run good --image=curlimages/curl --labels="access=granted" \
  -n demo --rm -it -- curl --max-time 5 backend
# Expected result: <!DOCTYPE html> ... nginx HTML is printed

# (b) Pod without label → blocked
kubectl run bad --image=curlimages/curl \
  -n demo --rm -it -- curl --max-time 5 backend
# Expected result: curl: (28) Connection timed out after 5000 milliseconds
tasks.explanation: NetworkPolicy allow rules are label-based. Because the condition is a label (access=granted) rather than an IP address or Pod name, the rule remains valid even after a Pod is rescheduled to a new IP — as long as the label matches. When default-deny-ingress and allow-to-nginx are both present, Kubernetes evaluates them as a union (OR). default-deny contributes 'no allow rules'; allow-to-nginx contributes 'allow TCP 80 from access=granted.' The union is 'allow TCP 80 from access=granted only.' The bad Pod in test (b) times out because it carries no access=granted label and therefore matches no allow rule.
hints: Writing podSelector and namespaceSelector inside a single list item (-) in the from block creates an AND condition (that Pod in that namespace). Placing them as separate list items creates an OR condition. A single level of YAML indentation changes the semantics entirely — pay close attention.
Run kubectl get networkpolicy -n demo to confirm that both allow-to-nginx and default-deny-ingress exist. If either is missing, the apply failed.
If the good Pod succeeds but so does the bad Pod, the CNI is not enforcing the policies. Run `kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep calico` to verify that the Calico Pods are in the Running state.

tasks.title: Advanced: Egress Control — Don't Forget to Allow DNS
tasks.goal: Write an egress NetworkPolicy that restricts the backend Pod's outbound traffic. Directly experience the DNS breakage that occurs when port 53 is omitted, then restore normal operation by adding the DNS allow rule.
tasks.prompt:
Step 1: Apply a deliberately broken egress policy (bad-egress.yaml) that omits the DNS port, then confirm that name resolution fails inside the backend Pod.

Requirements (bad-egress.yaml):
- Target: Pods labeled app=nginx
- policyTypes: Egress
- Egress rules: allow only TCP 5432 to Pods labeled app=postgres (no DNS rule)

Step 2: Delete bad-egress.yaml and apply a correct policy (allow-egress.yaml) that also allows DNS (UDP+TCP 53). Run the name resolution check again to confirm it now succeeds.

Hint: To run commands inside the backend Pod, use the form `kubectl exec -n demo backend -- <command>`.
tasks.starter:
# bad-egress.yaml — broken example with no DNS rule (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: bad-egress
  namespace: demo
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  policyTypes:
    - ____
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: postgres
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432

# Apply, then confirm DNS failure
kubectl apply -f bad-egress.yaml
kubectl exec -n demo backend -- ____   # run: curl --max-time 5 http://kubernetes.default

# -----------------------------------------------
# allow-egress.yaml — allow DNS + postgres (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress
  namespace: demo
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:                        # Allow DNS
        - namespaceSelector: {}
      ports:
        - protocol: ____
          port: ____
        - protocol: ____
          port: ____
    - to:                        # Allow postgres
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: postgres
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432
tasks.solution:
# bad-egress.yaml — broken example with no DNS rule
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: bad-egress
  namespace: demo
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: postgres
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432

# Step 1: Apply the broken policy and confirm DNS failure
kubectl apply -f bad-egress.yaml

# The nginx image does not include nslookup; use curl to check name resolution
kubectl exec -n demo backend -- curl --max-time 5 http://kubernetes.default
# Expected result: curl: (6) Could not resolve host: kubernetes.default
# → Port 53 is blocked, so name resolution itself fails

# -----------------------------------------------
# Delete the broken policy
kubectl delete networkpolicy bad-egress -n demo

# allow-egress.yaml — allow DNS + postgres
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress
  namespace: demo
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:                          # Allow DNS lookups (CoreDNS lives in kube-system)
        - namespaceSelector: {}    # All namespaces, including kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
    - to:                          # Allow communication to the postgres Pod
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: postgres
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432

# Step 2: Apply the correct policy and confirm DNS is restored
kubectl apply -f allow-egress.yaml

kubectl exec -n demo backend -- curl --max-time 5 http://kubernetes.default
# Expected result: DNS resolves; the response may be a 401 Unauthorized (API server auth required)
# → Name resolution succeeds, confirming the DNS egress rule is working correctly

# Verify the final policy list
kubectl get networkpolicy -n demo
# NAME                   POD-SELECTOR   AGE
# allow-egress           app=nginx      10s
# allow-to-nginx         app=nginx      5m
# default-deny-ingress   <none>         8m
tasks.explanation: Adding Egress to policyTypes switches that Pod's outbound traffic to whitelist mode as well. The most common mistake is forgetting DNS. Name resolution inside a Kubernetes cluster goes through the CoreDNS Pod in the kube-system namespace on UDP/TCP port 53. If port 53 is missing from the egress rules, the Pod cannot resolve any domain names and produces a 'Could not resolve host' error. The reason `namespaceSelector: {}` is used for DNS is that CoreDNS lives in kube-system, not in the same namespace as the backend Pod. An empty namespaceSelector means 'all Pods in all namespaces,' which includes kube-system's CoreDNS. In production, it is recommended to narrow the scope to `namespaceSelector: {matchLabels: {kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system}}`.
hints: The official nginx image does not include nslookup. Use `kubectl exec -n demo backend -- curl --max-time 5 http://kubernetes.default` to check name resolution. 'Could not resolve host' means DNS is blocked; 'Connection refused' or '401' means name resolution succeeded (the name resolved but the connection was refused).

If you apply allow-egress without first deleting bad-egress, both policies will be active and their allow rules will be unioned. Always run `kubectl delete networkpolicy bad-egress -n demo` before applying allow-egress.

The absence of an actual postgres Pod does not invalidate this exercise. Adding the app=postgres rule to the egress policy is simply a declaration: 'when a Pod with that label appears, allow it.' The key behavior to observe is the DNS allow rule.
